Precision, Decimal Places and Fractional Part of a Numeric Value

Source: R/precision.R
precision.Rd

nDec() returns the number of decimals.
prec() returns the precision of a number x, i.e. the smallest positional value of its last significant digit (e.g. 0.001 for 3.142).
frac() returns the fractional part of a numeric value.
maxDigits() returns the maximal number of decimal digits in x.

Usage

nDec(x)

prec(x)

frac(x, dpwr = NA)

maxDigits(x)

Arguments

x

the numeric value (or a vector of numerics), whose fractional part is to be calculated.

dpwr

power of 10 for a factor z, the fractional part will be multiplied with. The result will be returned rounded to integer. Defaults to NA and will then be ignored.

Value

  • nDec(): an integer vector of the same length as x; NA elements yield NA.

  • prec(): a single numeric value, the finest precision found across all (non-missing) elements of x. Returns 1 if all values are zero and NA if no non-missing values are left.

  • frac(): a numeric vector of the same length as x.

  • maxDigits(): a single integer value.

See also

format.info, as.integer, trunc

Examples


x <- rnorm(5)*100
x
#> [1] -155.70357  192.31637 -185.68296 -210.61184   69.76485
frac(x)
#> [1] 0.7035744 0.3163653 0.6829628 0.6118436 0.7648527

# multiply by 10^4
frac(x, dpwr=4)
#> [1] 7036 3164 6830 6118 7649

maxDigits(c(1.25, 1.8, 12.0, 1.00000))
#> [1] 2

x <- c("0.0000", "0", "159.283", "1.45e+10", "1.4599E+10" )
nDec(x)
#> [1] 4 0 3 2 4
prec(as.numeric(x))
#> [1] 0.001
